data.title: Has Synchrony Financial’s 2025 Rally Fully Reflected Its Credit Partnership Expansion?
data.description:
• Wondering if Synchrony Financial is still a smart buy at today’s price, or if you have already missed the best of the upside? You are not alone. This piece is going to dig into what the numbers really say about its value.
  • The stock has climbed 5.2% in the last week, 13.6% over the past month, and is now up 29.6% year to date, with a 184.7% gain over five years that has many investors asking whether it is getting stretched or just hitting its stride.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Synchrony’s ongoing expansion in co branded and private label credit partnerships and its push into more data driven consumer finance solutions, reinforcing the market’s view of it as a nimble player in retail focused lending. At the same time, broader conversations around consumer credit quality and interest rate expectations have added extra scrutiny to lenders like Synchrony, making its recent share price strength even more noteworthy.
  • On our framework of six valuation checks, Synchrony scores a strong 5 out of 6. This suggests the stock still looks undervalued on most of the usual metrics. Next we will walk through those methods in detail, while also touching on a more holistic way to think about valuation that we will return to at the end.

Synchrony Financial delivered 25.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Consumer Finance industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Synchrony Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that investors reasonably demand on its equity, and then projects how long that advantage can last. For Synchrony Financial, the starting point is a Book Value of $43.99 per share and a Stable Book Value estimate of $50.61 per share, based on future book value forecasts from 9 analysts.

Analysts expect Stable EPS of $9.97 per share, derived from weighted future return on equity estimates from 11 analysts. With an Average Return on Equity of 19.69% and a Cost of Equity of $4.54 per share, the model calculates an Excess Return of $5.42 per share, meaning Synchrony is projected to earn well above its cost of capital on each dollar of equity.

When these excess returns are capitalized over time, the model produces an intrinsic value of about $145.46 per share. Compared with the current market price, this implies the stock is roughly 41.9% undervalued, which suggests investors are not fully pricing in Synchrony’s ability to compound excess profits.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Synchrony Financial is undervalued by 41.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 907 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Synchrony Financial.

Approach 2: Synchrony Financial Price vs Earnings

For a consistently profitable lender like Synchrony, the price to earnings, or PE, ratio is a useful way to gauge how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of current earnings. In general, companies with stronger growth prospects and lower perceived risk can justify a higher PE, while slower growth or higher risk should pull that multiple down.

Synchrony currently trades on a PE of about 8.7x, which is slightly below the Consumer Finance industry average of roughly 9.2x and far below the broader peer group average of around 59.4x, a level that is skewed by higher growth or more speculative names. To refine this view, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 15.1x, which estimates the multiple Synchrony should trade on given its earnings growth outlook, profitability, risk profile, industry positioning and market cap. Because it explicitly blends these fundamentals, the Fair Ratio offers a more tailored benchmark than blunt peer or industry comparisons.

With the Fair Ratio of 15.1x sitting well above the current 8.7x, the PE analysis points to Synchrony trading at a meaningful discount to where it arguably should be.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Discounted Cash Flow model looks at the cash Comstock Resources is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those projections back to what they are worth in $ today. It is essentially a way of translating future free cash flow into a single present value per share.

Comstock’s last twelve months Free Cash Flow was negative at around $396 million, reflecting a period of heavy investment and weaker gas pricing.
